figures.caption: Figure 8: Streaming rollouts on LIBERO and VLA-arena. Default vs. Premover on a shared time axis aligned to the start of typing. Default idles through the typing window; Premover commits early via the readiness gate and finishes earlier. Green overlay shows focus-map patches with probability ≥ 0.8 (cf. App. C).

Figure 2: Conventional VLA inference vs. Premover. Conventional VLAs (top) idle during instruction input—nearly 40% of total interaction time—before running a forward pass. Premover (bottom) conducts forward passes during the user’s input, so that the focus map highlights the target referent in the streaming prefix and a learned readiness gate decides when to commit to actions, reducing end-to-end latency to 86.4% of the full-prompt baseline on LIBERO.

Figure 6: Qualitative focus maps on LIBERO. Per suite (rows): RGB observation, focus-map overlay (probability ≥ 0.8), and target instance mask. Above-threshold patches localize the target and frequently extend onto adjacent affordance regions (handles, lids, contact surfaces).
Figure 7: Qualitative focus maps on VLA-arena. Same layout and threshold as Fig. 6; the affordance-extension pattern persists under VLA-arena’s distribution shift.

Table 1: LIBERO results. Per-suite success rate and end-to-end wall-clock time, evaluated over 350 rollouts per suite. Percentages in parentheses report wall time relative to the full-prompt baseline, where full-prompt is 100%. Wall-Clock Time (All) averages over all rollouts; Wall-Clock Time (Succ.) restricts to successful rollouts. Lower wall-clock time is better. The full-prompt baseline’s wall-clock time includes the typing interval (Appendix D). Naive premoving collapses on success (95.0 → 66.4%); Premover recovers to 95.1% while reducing mean all-rollout wall-clock time to 86.4% of the full-prompt baseline.

Table 2: VLA-arena Level-1 suites results with π0.5 on selected episodes 15–49. Same convention as Tab. 1. Wall Time (Succ.) on Long Horizon is reported as ‘–’ since success is 0% for all three settings. Naive premoving suffers a 6.0%p success drop (33.0 → 27.0%); Premover preserves more of the success rate (30.9%) while reducing mean all-rollout wall time to 89.7% of the full-prompt baseline.

Figure 3: Premover overview. Two trainable projection heads, attached to a frozen VLA backbone, produce a focus map (3.1) by comparing image patches with streaming-prefix tokens in a shared latent space. The map at step t reweights image tokens at step t+ 1, and an action readiness gate—a learnable scalar threshold τ on the readiness score rt—decides when the policy starts acting.
